content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CorivIMIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
April 24, 1998
LeeAnn M. Peiham
Deputy Director
Los Angeles City Ethics Commission
201 No. Los Angeles St.
L.A. Mall-Suite 2
Los Angeles, CA 90012
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. G-98-122
Dear Ms. Petham:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Los Angeles City Ethics
Commission (“Commission”) about the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Your advice request
addresses conduct by third parties which has already occurred. Ordinarily, we do not provide
advice under such circumstances. (Regulation 18329.) However, when, as here, the request
comes from a governmental agency charged with enforcing ethics laws, and that agency has a
pending advice request which requires an interpretation of the Act, and the Commission has not
previously provided guidance on the issue, an exception is warranted.
I. QUESTIONS
A. Did Mr. Depoian receive a gift, within the meaning of the Act, when he accepted the
business-to-first class upgrade from the airline?
B. Did Ms. Kramer receive a gift when Mr. Depoian transferred the upgrade to her? If
so, from whom, the airline or Mr. Depoian?
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. G-98-122
Page 2
II. CONCLUSIONS
A. Mr. Depoian did not receive a gift from the airline when he was assigned the upgrade.
B. Ms. Kramer received a gift from Mr. Depoian when she accepted the first class
upgrade from him.
Ill. FACTS
On March 7, 1998, a Los Angeles trade delegation accompanying Mayor Richard
Riordan arrived at CKS Airport in Taipei, Taiwan and was ushered into a holding room. The
delegation’s passports and airline tickets had been previously gathered by protocol staff
accompanying the group. This procedure of collecting tickets and passports for large groups
traveling together was followed at all the airports throughout Asia.
When the tickets and passports were returned, Mr. Phil Depoian, a city employee and
member of the delegation, who had purchased a business class seat, noticed that he had instead
been assigned to a first class seat. He was alarmed that a mistake had been made in the purchase
ofhis ticket by city staffwho arranged his trip (city employees are not permitted to fly first
class). He checked all of his travel papers and ascertained he had indeed only purchased a
business class ticket. He then went to the ticket agent/clerk for the airline to point out what he
thought was a mistake. She communicated back to him that his seat assignment was correct.
Since he preferred to sit with a work colleague in business class, Mr. Depoian prevailed upon
Ms. Kramer to take the first class seat assigned to him.
After this matter received media attention in Los Angeles, EVA Air investigated the
circumstances surrounding the first class seat assignments on that particular flight. It determined
that the business section seats on the flight were overbooked, and that personnel on the ground at
CKS Airport made reassignments at random, without regard to any individual’s status, to
alleviate this problem. It thus appears the airline had to reassign seats for its own purposes.
Other business class travelers who were not in the Los Angeles trade delegation were also
assigned to first class.
The attorney requesting your advice on behalf of Ms. Kramer pointed out that
Mr. Depoian was traveling with co-workers with superior status and title to him, and that,
apparently, in the Taiwanese culture, it would be extremely inappropriate to honor an official
with lesser ranicing in a situation where a superior was not so honored. She also asserted that this
seems to confirm that the EVA Air officials on the ground at the airport who were upgrading
tickets were unaware of the nature of the group traveling or the official status of any individuals.


File No. G-98-122
Page 3
The travel agent in Los Angeles who originally booked Ms. Kramer from Los Angeles to
several Asian destinations, using what is called a “circle fare” structure, recently attempted to
value the Taiwan to Los Angeles leg using a first class fare (this was difficult after the fact). She
concluded that the difference in cost between business class and first class on that leg was $772.2
IV. ANALYSIS
The Act’s definition of “gift” is found in Section 82028. Particularly relevant here is that
the definition of gift “includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the
rebate or discount is made in the regular course ofbusiness to members of the public without
regard to official status.” (Section 82028(a).)
The business class section on the flight was overbooked. EVA Air reassigned business
class passengers to first class for a business reason: to mitigate the overbooking. The
reassignment was random and made without regard to Mr. Depoian’s status as a member of the
delegation. Under these circumstances, Mr. Depoian received a discount in the price of a first
class ticket, which is certainly something ofvalue, but he received it in the airline’s normal
course ofbusiness, and apparently without regard to his official status. Thus, EVA Air’s
selection of Mr. Depoian for a first class upgrade did not fall within the Act’s definition of “gift.”
However, receipt of the upgrade did result in Mr. Depoian being in control of something
of value. He chose to transfer it to Ms. Kramer, his superior. This transfer was a gift, unless
some exception applies.
In the Morrissey Advice Letter, No. 1-94-400, the advisee was a legislator whose son was
an airline pilot. The son received free “standby passes” as an incident of his employment. The
son gave the passes to his father, the legislator. We concluded that the legislator had received the
gift of the passes from his son, not the airline. We based this conclusion on the facts that there
were no circumstances to indicate that the airline intended to give the passes to the father, and
that the use of the passes was controlled by the son as part of his employment agreement.
2 Nothing in this advice letter should be construed as a determination by the Commission that any
particular valuation of the upgrade is correct or incorrect. However, under these bizarre (and hopefully unique)
circumstances, the Commission would accept a reasonable valuation that would avoid unjust enrichment of
Mr. Depoian by a co-worker.


File No. G-98-122
Page 4
The passing of the upgrade to Ms. Kramer was a gift from Mr. Depoian to Ms. Kramer
because it was a transfer
3 that conferred a personal benefit on her (i.e., travel in the relatively
greater comfort of first class), and for which Mr. Depoian did not receive consideration of equal
or greater value. (Section 82028(a).) No exception to the definition of “gift” applies.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
G
eral Counsel
(L4’
By/
John ‘ergeili
Staff (jounsel, Legal Division
SGC:JV:tls
The statutory defmition of gift refers to a “payment.” (Section 82028(a).) The Act’s definition of
“payment” is very broad, and includes “a ... transfer ... of... anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible.”
(Section 82044.) Thus, Mr. Depoian’s transfer of the upgrade to Ms. Kramer was a “payment,” within the meaning
of the gift definition.
